title: Maximum Depth of a Tree
description:
### Objective
Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth, defined as the number of nodes along the longest path from the root down to a leaf.

### Function Contract
Implement `__init__(self: any, val: any, left: any, right: any)` in Python.
Do not change input/output semantics expected by the tests.

### Input and Output
- Input `tree`: type `array` (example: `[3,9,20,null,null,15,7]`).
- Return type: `number`.

### Requirements
- Return the exact expected output for every test case.
- Handle edge cases (empty input, duplicates, boundary values) without raising exceptions.
- Target complexity: time `O(n)`, space `O(h) recursion`.

### Constraints
- 0 ≤ nodes ≤ 10^5
- Node values are 32‑bit signed
- Tree may be empty
explanation:
Intuition
The depth of a node equals one plus the maximum of the depths of its children. A simple postorder traversal naturally computes this bottom‑up.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Recurse to leaves computing depths
2. Main Algorithm: Take max of left and right and add one
3. Return Result: Return the value to parent

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n)
- Space Complexity: O(h) recursion

Key Insights
- Postorder matches bottom‑up definition
- An empty subtree has depth 0

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting base case for None
- Confusing edges vs nodes when defining depth
hints: Depth is nodes count not edges
